title: Requesting a “No” Vote on Senate Bill 1859
author: Russell Hunter
orientation: An open letter from Free the States to the Oklahoma Senate Health and Human Services Committee urging members to reject SB1859 — a heartbeat/professional-conduct bill being floated as a substitute for the abolition bill SB13. Russell Hunter argues SB1859 is unjust, unconstitutional, doomed in the courts, and being used to delay abortion's total and immediate abolition.
audiences: Oklahoma state senator weighing how to vote on SB1859
Pro-life Christian who currently supports incremental legislation
Abolitionist activist organizing constituents to call legislators
Pastor or church leader trying to understand the SB13 vs. SB1859 dispute
Journalist covering the Oklahoma abortion debate

questions.question: Why does Free the States insist that SB1859 is not what Oklahoma Christians and Republicans were asking for?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader trying to understand the SB13 vs. SB1859 dispute
questions.answer: Multiple Oklahoma religious and political bodies — the Oklahoma Baptists, the Oklahoma Association of Free Will Baptists, and the Oklahoma State Republican Party — had recently amended their platforms to demand abortion's total and immediate abolition, and the thousands who rallied at the Capitol were demanding SB13. SB1859, which only tweaks health codes and definitions of professional conduct, falls far short of that demand.
questions.quote: Senate Bill 1859 is not what the Oklahoma Baptists, the Oklahoma Association of Free Will Baptists, and the Oklahoma State Republican Party had in mind when they all passed amendments to their respective platforms in the past nine months calling for abortion’s total and immediate abolition. They were demanding Senate Bill 13.

questions.question: What does Hunter accuse the committee of doing by advancing SB1859?
questions.audience: Journalist covering the Oklahoma abortion debate
questions.answer: He charges that the committee has effectively begun killing SB13 — the abolition bill — by offering SB1859 in its place as a lesser substitute, and warns that Oklahoma Christians will not be appeased by the swap.
questions.quote: What you’ve done instead is you’ve begun the process of killing SB13 and you’ve offered a lesser substitute bill in its place. The Christians of Oklahoma will not be appeased or fooled by it.

questions.question: Why does the letter argue that compromising with abortion is no longer politically safe for legislators?
questions.audience: Oklahoma state senator weighing how to vote on SB1859
questions.answer: Hunter says the political paradigm has shifted: platform changes by Christian and Republican organizations and the massive Capitol crowd show that legislators will now be condemned, not praised, for compromising with abortion.
questions.quote: Compromise with abortion is iniquitous, but it’s also political folly. As demonstrated by the various organizations’ platform changes and the enormous crowd last Tuesday, the paradigm has shifted and you no longer will receive praise for compromising with abortion.

questions.question: On what biblical and constitutional grounds does Hunter say SB1859 is discriminatory?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who currently supports incremental legislation
questions.answer: Because life begins at fertilization — not at the detection of brain waves or a heartbeat — a bill that draws its line elsewhere fails to extend equal protection to every human being, violating both Scripture and the U.S. and Oklahoma Constitutions.
questions.quote: Life does not begin when an abortionist detects brain waves or a heartbeat, it begins at fertilization. By not defining life as beginning at fertilization, SB1859 is a discriminatory bill that violates God’s word, and by not providing the equal protection of the law to all human beings, SB1859 is in violation of the US and Oklahoma Constitutions.

questions.question: Why does the letter predict that SB1859 will never actually go into effect?
questions.audience: Oklahoma state senator weighing how to vote on SB1859
questions.answer: SB1859 contains no language defying federal court abortion jurisprudence, so it will be struck down under the “undue burden” standard. Hunter argues the only viable path is for the state to treat Roe and related rulings as legal nullities, since the Supreme Court has no authority to legalize murder.
questions.quote: SB1859 contains no language defying evil and unconstitutional court opinions. It thus has no chance of ever going into effect. The courts will rule SB1859 a violation of the “undue burden” standard and we all know it.

questions.question: What lesson does Hunter draw from Alabama's Human Life Protection Act?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian who currently supports incremental legislation
questions.answer: Even though Alabama's bill was widely cheered, it bowed to the Supreme Court and never took effect — evidenced by Planned Parenthood breaking ground on a new mega center in Birmingham only months later. The takeaway: pro-life bills that defer to the federal courts cannot stop the bloodshed.
questions.quote: The Alabama Human Life Protection Act may have sounded good to many when it passed last year, but because it bowed to the supreme court, Planned Parenthood broke ground on a new mega center in Birmingham just a few months after its passage.

questions.question: Why is it absurd, according to the letter, to trust abortionists to enforce SB1859's heartbeat or brainwave threshold?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist organizing constituents to call legislators
questions.answer: Even setting aside its other defects, SB1859 hands enforcement to the very people committing the murders — expecting abortionists to make a good-faith effort to detect a heartbeat or brain waves before proceeding.
questions.quote: Even if the bill weren’t unjust and certain to never go into effect, the bill trusts the murderers themselves to detect the heartbeat or brain waves, as if these are people who will make a good faith effort to do so.

questions.question: What is wrong with framing abortion as "unprofessional conduct"?
questions.audience: Journalist covering the Oklahoma abortion debate
questions.answer: Hunter calls the descriptor a "shocking understatement" — reducing mass murder to a matter of professional ethics is a failure of legislative duty, and merely revoking medical licenses is woefully inadequate to the scale of the killing.
questions.quote: “Unprofessional conduct” as a descriptor for abortion is a shocking understatement. To treat murder as something that is merely unprofessional is to fail utterly in your duties as a legislator.

questions.question: How does Hunter distinguish between legislators with bad intentions and those with good intentions who still back compromise bills?
questions.audience: Oklahoma state senator weighing how to vote on SB1859
questions.answer: He says some legislators are intentionally using incremental bills to appease Christians while blocking abolition, while others mean well but mistakenly think compromise is acceptable. He calls the well-meaning ones to recognize that incremental bills practically delay abolition and empower its opponents.
questions.quote: There are some in this legislative body who are intentionally supporting incremental compromise bills as part of a concerted effort to appease Oklahoma Christians while these very same people are the ones preventing abolition. There are also some who have good intentions but errantly think we should try to make compromises where we can make them.

questions.question: What posture does Free the States promise toward legislators who change course and support abolition?
questions.audience: Oklahoma state senator weighing how to vote on SB1859
questions.answer: Hunter pledges that abolitionists will not hold past disagreements against any legislator who repents and acts justly going forward — prior conduct will be "water under the bridge" for those who do the right thing.
questions.quote: We will not turn anyone who does the right thing away because of past differences. We will honor you if you do what is right and actions taken previously will be water under the bridge.

questions.question: What concrete action does the letter ultimately ask the committee to take?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist organizing constituents to call legislators
questions.answer: Vote SB1859 down and give SB13 a hearing — the letter frames voting down the substitute as the necessary first step toward stopping the bloodshed.
questions.quote: Senate Bill 1859 will be opposed by the Christians of Oklahoma. You should oppose it as well. Please vote against SB1859 and give SB13 a hearing.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
related.reason: Companion piece by James Silberman laying out the full case for the 'No Bill But SB13' strategy that this letter applies to SB1859.

related.title: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
related.reason: Direct follow-up about the very committee chairman this letter is addressed to and his ongoing obstruction of SB13.

related.title: Contrary to Treat's Media Tour, SB13 is Not a Secession Bill
related.reason: Rebuts the leadership's public framing of SB13 in the same legislative cycle that produced SB1859.

related.title: Anti-SB13 Campaigners Aren't Ignorant. They're Trying to Deceive You.
related.reason: Reinforces Hunter's claim that some legislators intentionally use incremental bills to keep abortion legal while appearing pro-life.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Generalizes the four objections Hunter raises against SB1859 into a broader abolitionist critique of incrementalism.

related.title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
related.reason: Documents one of the platform changes Hunter cites to argue the political paradigm has shifted in Oklahoma.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Profiles the legislative gatekeepers — including the chairman addressed in this letter — who have repeatedly blocked SB13.
